Zocdoc is terrible and I’m sure the…. Clinicians running small practices - beware of this company! They charge providers ridiculous amounts of money to be listed on their platform so the only people on Zocdoc aren’t necessarily good providers - but people who can afford to pay to be on their platform. I’m seeing a lot of zocdoc commercials on tv lately and it’s a bummer because my experience with them as a provider was so disappointing and felt so exploitative. If you run a small healthcare practice DO NOT use this company. They will charge you even when clients no-show or cancel without any flexibility or compromise - I believe their rate is around $70 per “referral.” As a provider I have found this company to be very unsupportive and inflexible

Zocdoc? Unethical, prolly Illegal.. Zocdoc covers for unscrupulous providers, and they abound. I’ve had several providers with advanced credentials and many availabilities call me and before I can say anything, they say there are no such providers available. They offer providers with lesser credentials instead. Zocdoc responded to my ensuing complaint with flowery, sympathetic messages, saying 1).They would investigate (but never tell me the outcome or related consequences); and 2) They would connect me with a list of providers, if I gave some info. THE PROBLEM WAS, THOSE MESSAGES RAN IN LOOPS THAT DID NOT END. In other words, they were talking ZERO ACTION—and had no plans to. I think the strategy is to tire you out, or infuriate you to the point of saying something out of bounds (which I’d never do), so they could rule out service. It’s unethical. And I’m guessing, it’s illegal

Fake Reviews, Awful Website. My husband saw a doctor in February 2025. We had a bad experience with him  and I wanted to leave a review on Healthgrades to warn potential patients of him but I struggled to find the words. This doctor only had 1 star reviews until August 2025. Then out of nowhere he had a dozen 5 star reviews spread throughout 2025 that were not there before. With Healthgrades the date you post a review is the day it shows it was left so there's no way these people could have posted these reviews. Someone couldn't post a review from April 2025 in August 2025. So how did these reviews just suddenly appear? Healthgrades is lying about not posting fakes reviews and they won't let anyone post a negative review even if it's not personally attacking the doctor. They even state on their website that doctors can't pay for good reviews or to have negative reviews removed and it's all just blasphemy. Do not use this website to find good doctors because nothing on it is true

My brother just phoned and talked about his cervical injection procedure.  I am very upset for him due to the unprofessional treatment he was given.  Michael said that he was all prepared to go in for the injection...prepped, changed into the hospital garment...etc., when the nurse/office came in and questioned him continually about his transport to/from (which was provided by the VA) & questioned him about who was waiting for him at home.  Michael told them his son was waiting for him at his residence and the nursed proceeded to say that they could not confirm this, and that the VA vehicle needed to have a lift??  The nurse then said he had 2 choices: 1) Get the injection with no sedation or 2) schedule another appointment!!!  Michael was never informed of these extra requirements, drove all the way to this location from Cullom and also wasted the VA's precious time needed for other veterans. They had no compassion for his time wasted and the unprofessionalism at this location is horrible.  My brother will now go through the VA hospital because he is a veteran and will be treated with respect, compassion and the professionalism a veteran deserves!

The problem started when my prescription was sent to the pharmacy. It turned out the medication required prior authorization from my insurance, and I was told Teladoc does not handle prior authorizations. I then spent several days trying to get help and was unable to reach the provider to discuss alternatives while waiting. The request was marked urgent, but I was still left without a solution.

I understand that prior authorization requirements are determined by insurance plans and that providers may not know every plan’s requirements. However, patients should be aware that this can happen. My recommendation is to check your insurance formulary or prescription coverage before ending the appointment, especially if the medication is expensive or brand-name, so you can ask about alternatives if prior authorization may be required.

Surprise Medical Bill After Being Told the Appointment Was Free. When I made an online appointment to renew my prescription, I saw it showed me $0.00 charge. However, a month later, I received a surprise bill for $70 after insurance.
When I called customer service, they told me it was billed as a counseling appointment and that charges apply when prescriptions are issued by that type of provider. If I had known there would be a charge, I would have scheduled an in-person visit with a doctor instead and had my blood pressure and heart rate checked as well.
It feels very misleading that I was not informed of any cost upfront and only received a bill a month later. Had I been aware of the charge, I would have chosen a different type of appointment.
This feels like a bait-and-switch situation. Patients should be clearly informed of any charges before an appointment so they can make an informed decision about their care

Repeated Missed Appointments. I chose MDLive for the convenience and accessibility of online therapy, but my experience has been very disappointing.

Repeated disruptions like this make it difficult to build trust and maintain continuity of care.

I understand that emergencies happen, but when missed appointments become a pattern, it feels unprofessional and disrespectful of a patient’s time. I hope MDLive takes reliability and accountability more seriously when it comes to mental health services.

Unfortunately, based on my experience, I would not recommend MDLive or provider Catherine L. Kendrick for therapy

Greedy & Unhelpful. It is very obvious that providers do not care about patients at all, or to them (customers). Providers have had gaps in basic medical knowledge as well. 2 Separate Incidents:
1) The reason for my appointment was a provider is required to order lab test order for a specific diagnosis, and despite having provided this information to the pre appointment form this, I was not informed that an MDLive urgent care provider could not order lab tests until AFTER I was charged. I found this weird because urgent care centers can ALWAYS order lab tests. Reached out to customer service but to no avail for 5 months. Anyway, decided to give them another chance 

2) Provider genuinely could have been on a beach, there was so much background noise made it hard to hear them. I informed the provider of test results & still they prescribed me medication for something I tested negative for reasoning that it could have been a false negative. I was advised by my actual PCP and healthcare team to NOT take the medication since I did not have a confirmed diagnosis for the illness and it could bring more harm than good. 

Lesson learned. Always go in person to trusted & competent healthcare professionals. MDLive does not care

Save Time, Skip MDLive, Go See PCP or Urgent Care. This was one of the most frustrating experiences I've ever had with a doctor. I've been having ear pain in my right ear after a bad bout of seasonal allergies. This hasn't happened to me before, so I wanted to see if maybe it was an ear infection that needed antibiotics or if it would likely go away on its own. When it was time for my appointment, the doctor simply logged in for about 2 minutes, told me it's "probably" not an infection, and recommended that I go get it checked out with an urgent care. I live in a rural area where we don't have access to an urgent care, and I was only trying MDLive because my PCP is on vacation this week. So, for any perspective patients, I'd advise skipping MDLive and just paying the co-pay to go to an urgent care or your PCP. I feel like I just wasted an hour of my life and my ear still hurts

I am writing to provide feedback…. I am writing to provide feedback regarding a recent telehealth encounter that I believe reflects a significant gap in clinical judgment and patient care.

I sought a telehealth consultation after developing acute throat pain that began on Thursday and progressively worsened. The pain initially localized to the right side of my upper throat and quickly became diffuse and severe, making swallowing extremely difficult. By Saturday, I was experiencing significant pain requiring frequent doses of ibuprofen, along with a low-grade fever. I also reported a known exposure to a confirmed case of streptococcal pharyngitis.

Despite clearly communicating these symptoms and risk factors, I was advised that adults have a near zero likelihood of contracting strep throat due to immunity, and that treatment was not urgent. I was told I would need confirmatory testing before any antibiotics could be prescribed.

Following this encounter, my symptoms continued to worsen. I attempted to obtain care through my primary wellness center without success and ultimately sought treatment at an urgent care facility. A rapid strep test confirmed the diagnosis, and I was prescribed antibiotics. Within hours of starting treatment, my symptoms improved dramatically, and by later that same day, my throat pain was largely resolved.

This outcome highlights a missed opportunity for earlier intervention that could have significantly reduced my pain and functional impairment. While I understand the importance of antimicrobial stewardship, the clinical picture I presented—acute onset, severe odynophagia, fever, and known exposure—should have warranted stronger consideration for empiric treatment or, at minimum, more nuanced guidance.

Additionally, I later became aware of a similar case within my household, where another telehealth provider prescribed treatment promptly based on comparable symptoms and exposure history. This inconsistency raises concerns about variability in clinical decision-making across providers.

My intent in sharing this feedback is not simply to express frustration, but to encourage improvement in telehealth assessment protocols. Patients rely on these services for timely care, especially when symptoms are severe and access to in-person evaluation is limited. Greater attention to clinical context, risk factors, and patient-reported severity could help prevent unnecessary suffering and delays in treatment.

I hope this experience can be used as a training opportunity to improve diagnostic reasoning and patient-centered care in future encounters

I had a very disappointing experience…. I had a very disappointing experience with MDLive. I completed their online consultation form, submitted the requested information, and uploaded photos of my throat. I reported a severe sore throat and ear pain, but I was told I likely had a cold. I scheduled an urgent MDLIVE video…. I scheduled an urgent MDLIVE video visit with Dr. Aquilla Bashir Moosani, MD for my 4-year-old son, who was having persistent barky coughing despite asthma treatment and supportive care at home. I paid $40 expecting a professional pediatric evaluation. What I received was neither safe nor appropriate.

Dr. Moosani never asked to visually assess my child — not once did she ask to see him breathe, hear his cough, or even verify his color or distress level. This was a video visit for a respiratory complaint in a preschool-aged child, and no visual exam was done. Instead, she spent barely two minutes on the call and suggested I “double up on his Breyna inhaler.”

As any parent of an asthmatic or croup-prone child knows, Breyna (Symbicort) already contains a steroid and long-acting bronchodilator. Doubling the dose without assessment can irritate the throat and cause side effects — especially in a 4-year-old. When I questioned this, she offered no explanation, and the visit abruptly ended.

This was not a safe or thorough evaluation. No questions about prior medications, no review of his recent steroid course, and no acknowledgment of my detailed care notes that were uploaded before the visit.

As a mother who has worked closely with multiple pediatricians, neurologists, and specialists for my son, I was shocked by the lack of attention, empathy, and professional diligence. I would strongly caution parents against relying on this provider for pediatric respiratory issues.

MDLIVE should ensure that their pediatric doctors perform actual assessments before giving dosing advice — especially when it involves inhaled steroids. I am requesting a full refund and a review of this encounter for quality and safety compliance.

Parents — always trust your instincts. If a provider won’t look at your child, they haven’t truly evaluated them

10/10 DO NOT RECOMMEND. First, let me start by saying their policy for rescheduling or cancelling your appointment has to be done prior to 24 hours before your scheduled appointment time or you are charged $50. You will also be charged if you "miss" an appointment even if it's a technical issue on the their end. 

 I had a therapy appointment scheduled for this morning. I logged into the portal ten minutes before my appointment time. At five minutes till there will be a blue "join the call" button. This appeared and then I wasn't able to click to join. I refreshed the page, exited the browser, tried again, even tried from my phone, and nothing worked. I called the contact number because by this time it was into my appointment time and I couldn't access it. I didn't want to be charged for something that was clearly a technical issue on their end. I was told that since I had opened a ticket for the issue I would not be charged. THIRTY minutes after my appointment time was supposed to start I got an email from by bank about the charge being attempted. I immediately called them back and they "couldn't see where the charge had been attempted on their end". They also don't have a way to contact the provider to let them know what was going on and that this wasn't just me not showing up to an appointment. Customer service wasn't any help. They told me they would open another ticket, make it a priority, but that they really couldn't tell me if I was going to continue to be charged or not. I just feel like if the issue on their end I shouldn't be charged. Also, I didn't just not show up or cancel my appointment, I was unable to login through their website because of an issue on their end. How is that my fault? I also have a screenshots and videos of the issue so that could see what was actually happening. My last call ended with the rep telling me she would send me an email so I could send the the documentation to see if it would be acceptable proof of the incident. It's been 3 hours since that call and I still haven't received an email. I get that technical issues happen. I'm not upset at that. I am upset that they don't care that it was their error and are making me pay for something I had zero control over. It's bad enough that if someone had a true emergency and had to reschedule or cancel within 24 hours of their appointment that they would be charged as well, but when the issue is on their side and they are punishing the patient for that - that's wrong. I have had other issues with them, trying to schedule with providers and the providers cancel the appointments, or send a request to a provider for availability and no one responds.I even had a provider be rude and tell me "I was wasting her time" because I didn't know where I needed to start with therapy, just that I wanted to start it.  So this is actually just the final straw for me, and I will no longer use them. It's provided through BCBS and yes, it's an easy way to make therapy appointments but I'll find somewhere else to go. If this is the way they treat their patients then I want nothing to do with them, and they will not be getting my business. 10/10 do not recommend

I would never use MDLife again but they did give me a refund.. After tracking my blood pressure for awhile and saw how high it was getting (175/103) I decided to book an appt with my PCP but she is booked for several weeks so I thought I should try to get a temporary prescription until I could see my doc. Since my daughter recently got a BP script from Teladoc I knew it was possible to get that kind of script from a telehealth visit. So, when I saw that MDLive is in my insurance network I thought I could get a temporary script from them until I see my doc. I filled out the pre-visit form in which I detailed my BP issue so the doctor would be apprised beforehand but when he called he asked me what I needed help with, so I recounted everything I had already written.  He proceeded to tell me that it was "outside his scope" to write me even a temporary script "because he isn't my PC doc"...WTH?? Does anyone have a primary care doctor at MDLive? Why do they even exist if that's the case?   He told me the usual BP advice that I need to cut down on salt, caffeine, etc...as if that was any sort of real help that someone would be willing to pay for. He then went on to say that if my BP got any higher that I should go to urgent care!!

Afterward, I called Teladoc and two other telehealth services to ask if prescribing BP meds is within their scope of things they prescribe and the answer was "yes" all 3 times!  

I have called customer service twice now. The first CS agent confirmed that the doctors ARE allowed to call in scripts for BP meds since they are not narcotics or restricted. Both times I have called customer service I have been told that my case is being "escalated" to a supervisor and someone would call me back soon...but so far NO CALLS! 

So, just know going in, since they aren't your primary care doctor, there's a good chance that they will take your money and DO ABSOLUTELY NOTHING ELSE!

9-8-2025: So I upgraded from a 1-star to a 3-star because a supervisor finally called me back and she was super professional and nice, gave me a refund and explained that the reason the doctor couldn't give me a prescription was because the virtual appt option I chose was for an "urgent care" appt and only a virtual call to a primary care doctor could have resulted in a script. That makes absolutely no sense to me and when I was scheduling the virtual visit it didn't ask which kind of appt I wanted. Also, I explained to her that another problem I have with this situation and her explanation is that I had to detail what my issue was IN ORDER TO SET UP THE APPT so, as I told her, he would have read and already known what I needed help with and shouldn't have chosen to make the call if he'd known that he wasn't going to be able to do anything to help me. They need to have it built into their system to reject appt's if the pre-appt information deems the appt incompatible with their protocols of what they won't treat

I would have rated it five stars, but.... I would have rated it five stars.  Last year was my first time using Dr on  Demand. Knowing that my last in person one was all talk and lab work, I liked the convenience.  However, since my insurance pays 100% for Dr on Demand, I never received a bill. I contacted included health and they told me that it was a glitch and that I shouldn't worry about it and wouldn't receive any additional collection notices.  About a month later I received another collection notice.  Contacted included health.  While waiting, I went to the collection site and found that the visit had been mis-coded as self pay.  This time they admitted that was the issue and resubmitted to my insurance.  Problem solved or was it?  After this received a notification from my insurance company about a claim with the lab that Dr on Demand had sent me to for testing.  The claim was processed as out of network.  I contacted included health and they asked if I had chosen the lab.  I couldn't remember after 6 months, but later found the conversation with Dr on Demand on the included health app.  It clearly shows that during the initial previsit that I was given a choice of labs, the closest was 45 minutes from home, but only 15 minutes from my work.  I checked the covered labs in the included health app and it didn't show anything close to me.  I contacted included health about this again with this information and they told me that with the new information, quality control would contact me within 5-7 business days.  After 7 business days I contacted them.  No helpful response.  Today, 11 business days later, I contacted the original person and was told that it was still under review and would be another 5 business days.  When I told them that I believe that they were just pushing it out so that I would give up, she just repeated what was said.  Seriously, we need single payer/universal healthcare

I had a very disappointing and…. I had a very disappointing and frustrating experience during my recent telehealth appointment with this provider. I scheduled the visit because I am experiencing significant work-related stress, severe sleep disruption, exhaustion, and emotional strain that has begun affecting my ability to function and work.

During the session I explained in detail that the situation at my job has escalated to the point where I have been missing work and feeling overwhelmed. Instead of acknowledging the seriousness of what I was describing, the provider repeatedly reframed workplace disciplinary actions as “positive feedback” and suggested I should simply use them to “do better next time,” rather than addressing the impact the situation was having on my mental health.

At several points I explained that I was extremely stressed, agitated, and at my breaking point with the situation. I also requested documentation confirming that I had attended the appointment, as I have been struggling this week and had missed work. The provider refused to provide even a basic doctor’s note or visit confirmation.

What made the interaction particularly upsetting was that when I expressed my concern about not receiving any documentation or support, the provider appeared to smirk during portions of the conversation. That reaction felt dismissive and unprofessional, especially given that I was explaining serious stress and emotional distress.

When I stated that I felt the interaction was being handled poorly and that I intended to report the experience, the provider abruptly ended the call.

Patients seeking mental health care should feel heard, respected, and taken seriously. Unfortunately, this interaction left me feeling dismissed and unsupported at a time when I was actively seeking help for significant stress and emotional strain.

I hope the practice reviews how patients in vulnerable situations are treated during telehealth appointments so that others do not leave feeling the same way

We had a very disappointing experience…. We had a very disappointing experience using Doctor on Demand for my minor daughter. On multiple occasions, her therapist failed to attend scheduled appointments with no notice, no call, and no follow-up. These repeated no-call/no-shows were disruptive and unacceptable, especially for a child receiving mental health care.

During our most recent appointment, I experienced technical issues accessing the platform and contacted Doctor on Demand immediately to report the problem. Despite notifying support in real time, I was still charged $129 for the appointment. Resolving this charge became another ordeal, involving nonfunctional payment links and repeated attempts to get help by phone and message with little meaningful support.

I was also alarmed by guidance provided early in our care. Due to my divorce, staff advised me to share my login credentials with my ex-husband so he could access our daughter’s care. This directly contradicts basic HIPAA privacy standards and placed my personal protected health information at risk.

Overall, our experience reflected poor communication, improper billing practices, and inadequate attention to patient privacy. As a result, we have discontinued using Doctor on Demand. I would not recommend this service, particularly for families seeking reliable and professional care for minors

Health tap is a tentacle. Health tap is a tentacle of the healthcare and health insurance industrial complex. At some point - perhaps a few decades ago -medical professionals in all areas of healthcare decided to throw out the Hippocratic Oath and replace it with their lust for higher incomes declaring conditions to be worse than they really are, which, of course, means more treatment and more income. I tried Health Tap just to see if it confirmed the conclusions I’ve stated above - conclusions I’ve come to from my own horrific experiences with doctors and hospitals over several decades. What the one star and two star reviews are saying is true. The advice that is given by the doctors is cursory and isn’t something that one can’t find on their own by doing an internet search on symptoms. and I’m not surprised that they are being over billed, and/or double billed, etc. etc. Also, the price that customers are paying for this cursory advice is atrocious. Those who know the reality of our “ modern“ healthcare, and health insurance system shouldn’t be surprised at the lack of ethics that is ubiquitous in these institutions or that they would move online as an easier way to make good money, without really doing much, if any, for many people, and make a bundle doing it. 

Sure enough, after explaining what I will call a “female problem” to one of Health Tap’s doctors I was simply told that I should go see a doctor in person. It wasn’t a condition that I needed to see a doctor about. So I realized that this is one trick that is used by some doctors in the network, believing that everyone that calls in for advice is ignorant, So without much concern to what subscribers are referring to why not simply tell them to do that (go see a real doctor in person). On the surface, perfectly legal, but in reality, unethical, because the telehealth visit was extremely short and Health Tap got their fee upfront. So after the consultation I immediately canceled my membership. If one looks very carefully at the wording of the various reviews on Trust Pilot, they will see that the one and two star reviews are very detailed in relating their experiences, and they seem to have for the most part, a common theme and an angry tone. The five star reviews are all bright and cheery. And, frankly, I believe very few of those glowing reviews aren’t fake. Another thing to take notice of is Health Tap’s responses to the negative reviews. With one or two exceptions they are all pretty much the same: “We are very sorry that you had such a negative experience. Please contact us” and so on and so forth. Those responses from Healthtap in my opinion are just as fake as most of the positive reviews. The movement by the healthcare industry to jump on the telehealth bandwagon is ginormous. Health tap could be more honestly named WEALTH TAP

Branding HealthTap. Your organization requests data that 'providers' willingly volunteer. Feed AI. Create better algorithms? Tapping into physician mindset understand that it's not a cash and carry trip, rather an understanding (through corporate), that docs intuitively respond to curious scenarios—often perplexing and heightening the challenge—A chess game of sorts where extraction is not only compelling, it's delightful. Brilliant. But at some point there's a juncture: How much time can I put into this? At least some fair equitable compensation for time—And, after all, that commodity cannot be replaced. Get a trophy get a prize get a pat on the back and blah blah blah. "You're number one or two," and wisely constructed a system of delivering healthcare information at a fee. Hey. I've been doing this a long—four decades—and sure, I'll be inclined to help where I can when I can. But you folks charge patients a fee—At this point a jolt of 'what am I doing this for?" pops in. You are using docs/providers/prescribers without considerations. Clearly YOUR time is worth something, but not the good natured, dedicated people on the front line. I don't know. You're a business functioning to remain solvent, perhaps a low hanging fruit to be picked up by VC. But my view is you are using willing 'providers' at no cost. There are better ways to retain participants to make this company function optimally with clarity, and due participation in the machinations. The business model is simple enough to understand: Offer good hearted 'providers' to provide data which you can parlay into a databank usable by —Including but not limited to: Insurance companies, pharma, patients with 'doorknob' questions, and, by consensus gather enough material to market as a 'go to' site that if you've got a health related issue, just tap in about health—HealthTap. Cute name, but after a span of time HT is not a household ideation, rather a second, second thought. But a few bucks here and there from staging patients with issues…No no no, that's not where the serious capital comes from. Gather enough data and you can sell the whole kit and caboodle - Create an AP, take it public (excuse me, I'm getting ahead of myself). You have remarkably talented physicians of all specialties who pour over the material and -advantageously tickle them with a carrot that's not a carrot it's an internal motivator to help people. Whoever the MD MBA advisor had an idea, you've got a databank to market…Sure, you get folks vying to provide information, maybe to market their own practices, which I doubt is central to board certified minds to feed the monkey (if you will the liberty of language). Packaged and shopped: Biggest databank on the planet. Got it. By taking the elemental ideals of good hearted people give them a nod and computer generated wink. Frankly, it has too many holes (as a marketable construct) to stand up over time. Still not branded. Still not part of daily life (globally) - Okay, I know folks who've given in and volunteered…However there are better ways to attract VC/branding/and universal acceptance as the "Go To" location on the web

Sleazy renewal model.. The service is relatively convenient.  Doctors are available within 6 hours usually, and they are ontime.  If they can't help you and send you to a doctor, they don't charge you.  However, the auto-renewal business model is a little sleazy.  If you subscribe, you are automatically signed up to auto renew.  You can't get a refund if they renew you automatically.  You can cancel (and you get a warning that you will lose care even if they just charged you for a year), but you will be RESUBSCRIBED to AUTORENEW if you book an appointment in the year that you paid for it.  So, to prevent auto renewal, you have to cancel the service every time you book an appointment

She addressed my concerns directly and sent the prescription to the local pharmacy. I went to the pharmacy the next day and it said I only had 1 prescription. DING!! DING!! DING! They followed up with a message directly to the pharmacist and called several times to resolve it. I was able to successfully get what I needed. When things go wrong it’s easy to jump on and give a 1 star review. Equally when things go right, you just move on like nothing happened, because your world was not disrupted. PlushCare: CAUTION!. Dr. Alexander Drew, who somehow has 4.5 stars on the app yet there is no way to rate people on the app or anywhere that I can find, had an appointment with me while I was working and I just decided to cash pay (around $160) because I did not have my insurance card & couldn’t leave work but was sick. I had a self-diagnosed UTI confirmed by a test I took from Walgreens, symptoms, and experience. I also had a sinus infection. 
Also self diagnosed, but I usually get maybe one per year and after about a month and symptoms were getting worse and persisting, and having had several sinus infections in the past, I knew what it was. I asked him for antibiotics that would cover both the uti and the sinus infection and knew ahead of time it was Levaquin that was the appropriate choice. I also mentioned that I typically take urogesic blue but was out and believe if I had it, I wouldn’t have the uti. With urogesic, I only needed to take it five or six times a year maybe when I felt like I had engaged in any activity or not drank enough water, swam in dirty water, or done something that would lead to a UTI. After getting urogesic, I went from 5 to 6 UTIs a year down to one every few years. I’m a pharmacist so I know that urogesic blue can help with prevention. I ran out of it and wasn’t able to take the urogesic blue and developed a UTI. I also know he prescribed the antibiotic twice on accident to two different pharmacies. This is a strong antibiotic with a black box warning regarding severe, disabling, and potentially permanent side effects, including tendon rupture, tendinitis, peripheral neuropathy, and central nervous system effects. Good thing I’m a pharmacist and knew not to take both. After a couple of days I messaged PlushCare about the urogesic blue thinking he possibly meant to call in urogesic and Levaquin, still giving him the benefit of the doubt, he said I would have to book another appointment because we never spoke about that. That is 100% not true. I requested the video or transcript of the call. They wouldn’t provide it, but did confirm that at least a transcript existed. The company refused to read the transcripts that they keep of these appointments where I do mention it. 

This company is just like every other company that is THE problem in healthcare right now, in my opinion. Fake reviews? I asked to be directed to the reviews of the physician so I could write one and not only could I not figure out how to review him, the company couldn’t tell me either.  Also, here is what it looks like when you click the “link” they provide to cancel your membership. (Since I can’t add the photo I will copy and paste the text)

Sorry, we couldn't find what you were looking for.

So I called. A grievance was filed. And I was told the $20 fee that is very covertly added to be automatically withdrawn from your account monthly, is not automatically added if you call to make the appointment vs using the app. But the whole point is using the app/online because that’s where you go to have your telehealth visit. This is misleading and wrong. This is only the first platform I will be addressing their standards and practices, and holding them accountable. As I said before I’m a pharmacist and I see how broken the healthcare system is and I work with a very vulnerable set of patients who this could easily be confused by Plushcare. As healthcare providers our oath is “first do no harm” and this could harm several vulnerable people. It could also help a lot of people if it was a patient care first, money later approach. But it isn’t in my opinion so ask the right questions and know how things work. While this would be a great app for the elderly, or the homebound, I would never trust this company to serve my own grandmother

They told me I was ineligible, promised a refund, and then charged me for 7 months.. In August 2025, a PlushCare representative called me to inform me I was ineligible for the service and medication I had requested. She assured me I would be refunded. I immediately logged into my account, cancelled my membership, and removed my payment method entirely.
I never received a refund. I never received a single email, invoice, or any communication from PlushCare again. I assumed the matter was closed.
Seven months later, I discovered they had been charging my credit card $19.99 every single month — plus an additional $99 charge the same day they told me I was ineligible. Nearly $260 in unauthorized charges on an account I was told was closed, for a service I was told I couldn't even use.
This isn't a billing error. This is a company that continued charging a cancelled account, with a removed payment method, with zero communication, for over half a year. When I tried to dispute the charges with my bank, I discovered PlushCare had been silently updating my card details through Visa's account updater system to keep the charges going.
I have filed a formal demand for refund and will be escalating to the CFPB and FTC if not resolved within 7 days.
Do not give this company your payment information

Over the last year I've been a regular…. Over the last year I've been a regular customer of Hims for their GLP-1 compounded product.  I never really had any issues until this month. The long and short is that Hims suffers from a logistical problem and likely compliance issues with the FDA, still...

Regarding the logistics, I've had numerous cold chain shipments sent to me without any issues. That changed this month and for absolutely no reason.  What I find curious is that Hims claims they do. It ship via the Postal Service and especially not to Post Office boxes.  Where I live, we do NOT have rural mail delivery. FedEx and UPS deliver to my door, but the postal service does not. 

I was asked to provide another shipping address and I gave them my physical street address as requested before, but now, they say that the GLP-1 would be shipped by the postal service and I needed to provide another address.  That being said, the only other address I have is a PO Box.  

This issue was never resolved and frankly, after reading some of the warning letters issued by the FDA to Hims/Hers, I am not reassured that anything they provided was completely safe or met the minimum requirements of FDA/CDER

My experience with Hims Weight Loss has been deeply disappointing and raises serious concerns regarding continuity of care.

I enrolled in and paid for a 360-day weight loss program, completed all required intake forms, check-ins, and program requirements, and followed the prescribed treatment plan exactly as directed.

The problem began when Hims failed to ship a scheduled medication refill. As my medication supply ran out, I repeatedly contacted support seeking assistance to maintain continuity of treatment.

What followed was a revolving door of customer service representatives and physicians. Over multiple interactions, I was required to repeatedly explain my situation to new providers who appeared unfamiliar with the history of my case. Despite numerous messages, no single provider or case manager assumed ownership of the issue.

Rather than addressing the immediate problem of maintaining continuity of treatment, discussions shifted toward changing medications, pricing alternatives, and partial refunds. While refunds may address financial concerns, they do not address the interruption of a medical treatment plan already in progress.

My concern is not merely customer service. It is continuity of care, care coordination, and accountability.

I encourage prospective customers to carefully consider whether a highly distributed telehealth model with multiple rotating providers is consistent with their expectations for ongoing medical management.

This review reflects my personal experience and is supported by documented communications with Hims

Amazon One Medical is trash. Amazon One Medical is trash. Don't waste your time or money. Providers do not care or listen to you. They shove you to an online assessment that takes 20 minutes to complete then they base their ENTIRE treatment plan (or lack of) solely off how you scored on the assessment. They completely dismiss everything you say, all of your lived experiences, and all of your symptoms are completely ignored. All they use for your treatment is the results of an online assessment. 

You can be perfectly healthy and intentionally fail the online assessment and they will prescribe you medication. Or, in my case, have debilitating symptoms that make it impossible to function, score average on the assessment and get denied treatment.

Moral of the story: don’t trust Amazon with something as dear as your health

Amazon One (Out of Five Stars) Medical Was the Worst Online Customer Experience I Have Ever Had. Amazon One Medical yielded my first negative experience with any Amazon product in my life thus far. I strongly recommend that Amazon management spin this one off because it is a potential brand destroyer.

The company is one of several innovators that basically draws a significant stream of income through deceiving its customers with limited attention by appearing to offer huge discounts on a particular service, but actually selling a similar sounding but quite different service, c.f.
Homeaglow. In this case, One Medical looks sells a "membership" that gives you "access" to telemedicine but not "insurance" itself so be aware. 

I signed up for it because it was advertised as a free two week membership offering access to telehealth services, which sounded close enough to insurance. I was hoping to get a cheap referral for a difficult to obtain through my primary health insurance provider. I was ultimately billed $412 for a 12 minute call with a nurse practitioner* who could make no prescriptions, but only make outpatient referrals - none of which were covered by primary health insurance provider. The customer service phone line was actually deactivated when I called it. Via calling Amazon's overarching health business helpline, I was able to get in touch a generally unsympathetic representative from Amazon One Medical. Essentially, you are upcharged if you report that you have insurance (??) and, if you call to complain, Amazon One Medical offers to charge you only the rate you would have if you were uninsured.

As a health-conscious customer, I would stay as far away from this product as possible if you are in need of discount medical services. If you must buy it, read all the fine print, and assume that you are dealing with an extremely cynical operator on the other side. I am not the type of person to negatively review products and try to be forgiving in general, but my overall customer experience here was so unpleasant that I felt compelled to leave this review in the public interest.

*The nurse practitioner I was referred to was perfectly fine given the constraints of the platform, which perhaps keeps this review at 1/5 rather than 0/5 or < 0/5

I've read the negative reviews. I've read the negative reviews. I just have to say that's not been my experience at all at One Medical. Maybe I'm just lucky. The reason I signed up with them was because of the horrible medical care I was getting "in the wild" -- appointments scheduled months in the future, sitting in waiting rooms for hours despite an early morning appointment, being hustled through a three-minute visit without resolution of the issue, recourse or followup.  It was why I stopped getting annual physicals for 20 years.

In fact, I saw it after I became a One Medical member but it was from referrals to outside specialists for a condition that my One Medical doctor caught and that a non-One Medical MD completely missed.

In July 2022, at my first visit with my current One Medical physician, my intent was to find out why I had discolorations around my ankles. She explained that it was probably lack of exercise and my COVID weight and how the legs are like a second heart to move fluids pooling in the legs. She was right about both but that's a digression.  She ordered a full blood panel and called me two days later to tell me that my PSA was elevated and that she was referring me to a urologist because she said it was a 50/50 indication of prostate cancer.

That's when the clown show started again with the larger medical establishment, particularly at Mount Sinai: dropped appointments (but not being told it was dropped until I showed up for it), doctors losing test results, refusing to do telehealth visits only to deliver 30 seconds of news, being railroaded into scalpel surgery when I was a candidate for non-invasive stereotactic radiation surgery. I stayed in touch with my primary care MD at One Medical as she helped me to navigate my way through the substandard care I was getting elsewhere, including me firing my unhinged and ethically-challenged surgeon fifteen minutes before being wheeled into the OR. I finally landed at Weill Cornell/NY Presbyterian, which did a fabulous job. Five stars for them too.

But it was literally a year of hell with outside practitioners from the time she notified me about my PSA until it was finally resolved at NY Presby. I really couldn't have done it without her. She's remained bullish about me getting at least semi-annual wellness visits with blood tests.

Last year, I fell on some funky concrete stairs while carrying a heavy pressure washer, landing on my knee. It was incredibly painful and my leg was turning every color of the rainbow.  I called One Medical. My doctor was on vacation so they sent me immediately to their Brooklyn Heights location to see a nurse practitioner who was concerned about "partition syndrome", referring me to NYU Langone's Level 1 trauma ER.  I don't know how she did it but as soon as I signed in I was sent directly to an ER doctor despite a waiting room full of patients for a sonogram and X-ray. It turned out not to be anything serious but that One Medical NP took enough interest in the case to push it through the bureaucracy elsewhere.

I understand that One Medical isn't a hospital or even a clinic. They're concierge GPs who mostly just watchdog your health. If it's anything serious you get sent elsewhere. It was a One Medical doctor who saw signs of potential brain trauma in a good friend of mine and referred him out to NYU. It turned out to be a glioblastoma and he didn't make it. But he was pleased with One Medical because they saw him that very day about his blurry vision when his regular doctor gave him an appointment two weeks away. 

I work in public health. I know the real standards of care in NYC. I've never had to wait more than two days to book an appointment at my Smith Street One Medical facility nor wait more than 20 minutes for that appointment.  And I always get my same doctor unless it's for  vaccination or blood draws, when it's appropriately an NP

TRASH!!!. TRASH!!!

Do not purchase a subscription period

Serious Concerns About Billing and Medical Record Accuracy. People read these like stories, so give them just a bit more:

I’ve been a member of One Medical for some time, and the difference in quality over the past year is hard to ignore.

What prompted this review was a billing issue that should have been straightforward. I was charged for injections that were never administered. I flagged it, expecting a simple correction. Instead, I was told their records are correct and that I’m responsible for the charges.

That response is what’s most concerning. If a provider can’t reconcile what actually happened during a visit with what’s documented, that’s not just frustrating, it raises real concerns about the reliability of their medical records.

This used to feel like a modern, patient-first experience. Now it feels operationally inconsistent and difficult to trust.

I’d encourage anyone considering joining to look closely at recent reviews and weigh whether this is the level of care and accuracy you’re comfortable with

Women's Health. I had a follow-up appointment at One Medical regarding perimenopause care after previously seeing Dr. Shin Jung Yoon. My appointment was scheduled for 1:00pm; I arrived at 1:01 and was brought back at 1:07.

During the visit, I clearly explained the two main reasons I was there and where I hoped to focus. However, much of the appointment was spent revisiting prior lab work, which I was told was not valid due to timing and fasting issues.

When asked about the prescribed medication, I shared that it was causing cramping and not providing the intended benefit. Despite this, the recommendation was to continue the same course of treatment with minor adjustments. I expressed that I did not agree and was hoping to discuss alternative options.

At approximately 1:32, I was told my appointment time was over and that I could discontinue the medication if I chose, review materials later, and return for another visit. When I shared that I did not feel the visit had been helpful, I was told that I was being “mean,” which I found inappropriate and unprofessional.

I left the appointment feeling dismissed, unsupported, and discouraged. Patients seeking care—especially for complex and often frustrating conditions like perimenopause—deserve to feel heard, respected, and partnered with in their treatment decisions

Inflated Billing and Zero Accountability – Avoid One Medical. I had a brief 10-minute Zoom visit at One Medical's Dublin, CA location on June 25, 2025, for a low-to-moderate complexity issue (common cold). I expected charges aligned with their publicly advertised cash rates of $99-$149 for standard visits. Instead, they billed me $549, adjusted it slightly to a $457.09 balance, and ignored multiple emails from me where I pointed out the overcharge, lack of advance notice/consent under the No Surprises Act, and inconsistencies with their own pricing.
Despite my request for a re-bill at the appropriate rate or further documentation, One Medical never responded—not even an acknowledgment. Months later, without resolution, they escalated the unpaid balance to a debt collector (Frost-Arnett), potentially harming my credit over a disputed amount. This feels like predatory billing practices, especially for a company that markets itself as patient-friendly and transparent.
One Medical's lack of response and aggressive collections tactics show a complete disregard for fair practices. Save yourself the hassle—look elsewhere for primary care

Avoid at all costs; Don't be fooled by the Amazon branding.. To summarize:

1. I was already happy with my existing medical coverage in Florida; I only joined One Medical because of its associated Amazon branding. As a happy Amazon customer of many years, continually impressed with its exceptional customer support, I wanted to give One Medical a try in support of the Amazon brand and its mission to help improve primary care. I never would have joined One Medical had the "Amazon" name not been attached to it.

2. Prior to joining One Medical, I verified with the One Medical sales rep that my medical insurance, AvMed Entrust, was accepted.

3. Furthermore, the One Medical website specifically listed "AvMed Entrust" as an accepted insurance plan.

4. Even though One Medical bills by group, not specific physician, I proactively chose a PCP within One Medical that was showing as 'In-Network' on the AvMed website, out of an abundance of caution.

5. Mythili Penugonda, MD, an available One Medical provider, was showing as "in-network" on the AvMed website.

6. Despite joining One Medical in October with available appointments that same month, I proactively waited on scheduling my virtual visit until November to ensure that Dr. Penugonda would be my AvMed PCP of record effective 01 November.

7. Days prior to my virtual visit, I uploaded my AvMed Entrust insurance card to my One Medical profile and was informed that I had a $100 copay.

8. On 03 November 2025, Dr. Penugonda (an internal medicine physician) and I had an approximately 15- to 20-minute virtual/remote visit via Zoom. It was just a conversation, and no procedures were performed (virtual appt).

9. The $100 copay was paid promptly after my initial virtual visit with Dr. Mythili Penugonda on 03 November 2025. 

10. After the visit was completed and my copay was made, I was then told by both One Medical and AvMed that the visit was "Out of Network."

11. There is nothing more I could have reasonably done as a consumer to ensure that the visit was in-network.

12. I would never knowingly see an out-of-network physician, especially since I have insurance and can walk down the road to my prior PCP, Dr. Bharath Radhakrishna, MD, at very low cost.

13. Images in support of the statements made herein were all provided within my One Medical message portal.

My name is Hanna, and I’m a physician assistant. Thanks for reaching out, and I’m happy to share information about next steps. I’ve also included general information about the virtual services that are included with your membership for ongoing support of your health and well-being. YOUR PRESCRIPTION REQUEST(S) On-demand care options, like our Discuss Common Conditions (Treat Me Now) and urgent video chat features, are great for short-term medications and medications that can be safely managed without close monitoring. Because …….. is used to treat a long-term health condition, I recommend contacting the provider who usually prescribes your medication for a refill, even if you’ve been taking the medication for a long time. If you don’t currently have a primary care provider, please let me know and I’d be happy to provide resources to help you locate a provider in your area. If you’re unable to reach your usual provider and would like us to consider sending a one-time, short-term prescription to last until you’re able to follow up with them, please answer the following questions for each requested medication: 1.	Please list the medication name, dose, and full instructions as printed on your prescription label and attach a photo of the label, if possible. 2.	What is the reason you were originally prescribed this medication? 3.	When did you start taking this medication? 4.	Who usually prescribes this medication for you (name and specialty), and when was your last visit with this provider? 5.	Could you tell me more about why you’re requesting a prescription today (e.g., unable to reach your usual provider, looking for a new provider)? 6.	Have you ever experienced any side effects? If so, please describe. 7.	When did you have your ……. last checked? Please attach a copy of your most recent results, if available. 8.	Please list any chronic or ongoing medical conditions you’ve been diagnosed with. Best, Hanna

To Whom It May Concern at One Medical,. To Whom It May Concern at One Medical,
I’m not sure it’s possible to feel more dismissed, more gaslit, or more infuriated by a healthcare provider than I have been by One Medical.
Let me be blunt: I waited nearly two years for One Medical to have an actual doctor available in-office. My condition is complex. I respect physician assistants and nurse practitioners—but in my case, I needed a doctor. And for two years, One Medical failed to provide one.
Fast forward to now: I attempted to refill a common antibiotic for diverticulitis, a condition I’ve been diagnosed with and treated for since 2022. One Medical told me they had no record of my diagnosis. None. Not the specialist referral. Not the tests. Not the prescriptions. Not the consults. Nothing.
This is beyond absurd. I saw a One Medical doctor five weeks ago who specifically prescribed medication for my diverticulitis. We had a full consult about my history with it. Yet when I called to renew that prescription, I was told—again—that there was no record of my condition, my medication, or any reason to refill it.
From August 25th to August 30th, I spent hours trying to get through to patient relations. Every call ended the same way: denial, deflection, and a suggestion to “just book another appointment.” That might work for someone in perfect health. But for me, dragging myself into an office just to refill a prescription I’ve already been given is not just inconvenient—it’s cruel.
And the worst part? I was treated like a nuisance. Every customer service rep, every clinical nurse I spoke to acted like I was irrational for asking them to do their job: to look at my records, to acknowledge my diagnosis, and to refill a medication that’s clearly documented across my specialist network—from pulmonology to cardiology. Everyone knows I have diverticulitis. Everyone except One Medical.
So here I am, stranded. The specialist who originally diagnosed me is no longer in business. And One Medical, the company that absorbed that practice, has erased my history and left me without care.
If you’re looking for a provider that will ignore your records, deny your diagnosis, and treat you like a burden—One Medical is the place. If you value your health, your time, and your dignity, stay far away.
I hope someone at One Medical actually reads this and contacts me. But based on your track record, I won’t hold my breath.
You’ve made my medical life hell.
Sincerely,
Edwin H. Balder

Don't go there. I was looking for a new PCP when mine retired.  The closest One Medical PCP was a 2-hr drive away, but I was hopeful that she would be a good fit.  I got there early -- because according to One Medical appointments start on time -- and had to wait about 15 mins.  When I did see the doctor, she rushed through my intake.  I have a complicated medical history and expected the first visit to be comprehensive so I could receive the best INDIVIDUAL care.  When I asked about continuing a medication regime I have been on for 25 years with great success, rather than saying  she would not prescribe these medications, she hid behind fuzzy language, saying things like she "wouldn't be comfortable" prescribing these meds.  No explanation for why.  I have a Ph.D. in molecular biology, have extensively researched my medications, and probably know more about their effects and risks than she does.  After 2 weeks of going back and forth over this issue, she persisted with the fuzzy language, and I have given up.  She had no basis for refusing to prescribe what I've been on for 25 years, no explanation as to what would occur should I suddenly go off this medication.  In short, I felt that her biases were paramount in her mind, over-riding what would be the best medical care for me.  Had she been definite that she would not prescribe what I wanted at that first visit, it would have saved both of us some time and I could have started looking for a new PCP again.  I won't be renewing my One Medical membership

My only challenge is how frequently my pre-scheduled visits get cancelled and how long out I have to rebook, but once I have those meetings I'm always pleased with my experience

Forward - NYC. I've been a member of Forward for about 3 or 4 years now and can say I really love it. I lost my insurance a little after COVID and was tired of the runaround to get insurance through the state and the lack of coverage/options. My doctor is awesome and really cares about my health being at it's prime. She's never pushed medications on me or made me feel like she didn't have my best interest at heart. The facility is nice and clean and you never wait to be seen after doing the mobile check in. The app and chat options are great and I always get a response extremely quickly. Any results for bloodwork get uploaded into my profile right away and they confirm the results. Everything is pretty seamless. I will say I think if you're looking for urgent care this might not be the place for you. If I'm correct these doctors also work at other hospitals so to make an appointment same day may not always be possible. In all if you're looking for preventative primary care doctor from a friendly compassionate team this is the place for you!

Great primary care!. I only have GoForward primary care. I love it! It’s affordable and I have access to questions 24/7 and if I need care, I can get in to see a doctor right away. Everyone is very friendly and helpful. I highly recommend it!
I’ve been pleased with the convenience…. I’ve been pleased with the convenience and knowledge of the Care Team. I would recommend Forward to others

Very good care and right there for issues!. I have never had a care team more hands on. They are right there for all issues. The doctors have been very good as well as their care team. The only thing they need is a little better communication between docs and team. But they are working on that

Love the doctor and the support team. Love the doctor and the support team. Always responds quickly to my messages and I can see a doctor quickly if needed. I highly recommend the Forward service if you want to get back to what health care should have been all along!

Preventive care. I love the responsiveness of the team that replies to messages in the app.

One of the doctors that I had a visit with is focused on preventive medicine; which is exactly what I was looking for! The doctors seem to be able to take a bit more time and go more in depth than the doctors I've seen previously

I have had a super positive experience with Forward. I believe Forward Medical Care is a fantastic way towards the near future.
Forward does a thorough overview of examining your health and specific needs in their first baseline visit.  Then, they send a personal care kit to you immediately after you join them so they can check your vitals on their app daily.  They create a chart of how you are making progress with your numbers.  This motivates me to pay attention to my health, continuously stay in charge of my commitment in one location, and be able to chat with them if I need feedback.   They set up and share the results immediately and transparently with their Patients. They provide immediate responses to my questions and concerns. They have gone far beyond what I expected from a new start-up. Their use of technology to quickly present the results is within immediate reach and somewhat heartwarming, knowing if I am okay or not okay in a matter of hours instead of days. They provide skin scans and other care plans that a routine doctor visit will not provide.  Their approach is to be proactive and preventive, not just prescriptive. They reduce paperwork by making most communication happen online via chat and email. Their lab technicians were also very skilled and caring.  I did not even feel the needle when they were taking my blood. Like any other startup, it takes time for a new way of care to become standard.  However, I can see Forward will be gaining much attention soon.  It can become the one place for a diverse set of medical care services.  I hope they can connect with local hospitals more in-depth for quicker information exchange and become an active integrated model to their services so they all work on a single source of database relating to a patient.  No matter where a patient is located, they can receive consistent and immediate care by accessing a single source via their phone.  I highly recommend transitioning into this model of care

Not worth the fees, terrible experience. I joined Forward thinking it would make healthcare easier.  I was wrong.  If I wanted quicker service, I could have rejoined Kaiser (joke, because Kaiser is super slow.). In person appointments for anything were over two weeks out.  They took 5 days to review a lab request just to tell me Forward would not be able to fully cover.  I could have completed all the labs myself much faster than the time it took for them to get back about coverage.  Then the appointments were 15+ days out.  I then asked for 90 day refills on my medications because it’s cheaper through my insurance.  They gave me 30 day supplies and told me they wouldn’t update it unless I sent in my blood pressure readings.  Basically punishing me like a child.  On top of all of that, their chat customer service was not empathetic to any of my concerns. I would give Forward less than one star if I could.  I feel like they are checking boxes for themselves and not overly concerned about what’s right for the patient

Male Health Visit Gone Right!. I recently had the pleasure of visiting the Forward Health Chicago location and couldn't be more pleased with my experience. From the moment I walked through the doors, I was met with a warm and welcoming atmosphere that immediately put me at ease.

Truly love this service!!. Truly love this service!!

I've been using Forward for about three months and it is absolutely refreshing how responsive they are at Forward. Forward is a fantastic organization set…. Forward is a fantastic organization set up to really help drive healthcare in a new preventative, data driven direction. My interactions with the doctors and staff are all top notch and I will continue to stay with Forward..!

My experience with Forward continues to excellent. it is so refreshing to have medical personnel that are not punching the clock for insurance companies. Give it a try, guarantee you will be healthier!

Forward-Miami. My experience has been great so far! I needed a new PCP because of recently moving to Miami from NYC. The things that I enjoy the most about Forward are (1) ease of communicating with health team through the app and online with the ability to see my main physician in person as desired (2) having a dashboard on my phone that tells me everything I now know about my health in one place, and (3) the proactive approach Dr. Meghna uses to keep you engaged with your health choices

Great for busy individuals. The fact that you can send a message and have a response within an hour is great. I love the ease of scheduling appointments and the undivided attention given when in conference with the doctor. Never rushed

Forward / Chicago. Forward has been a great complement to my regular HCP care. The app has worked great for me in providing timely communication, even after hours, when I needed it. The interactions with the doctors have been positive. My first actual on-site visit (non virtual)  just took place and it was a pleasure not being rushed in my time with my Forward doctor, also a big draw for me. I always have a plan and next steps. 

While this service might be redundant to your regular healthcare, Forward's goal is a proactive approach and provides continued follow-up as needed. It is an added expense but one that has proven beneficial for me so far

A better healthcare experience, but with a hefty price tag. I've been a Forward member for a few years now, and overall I'm very happy with the service. I signed up for Forward after becoming frustrated with how brief the appointments were at my regular doctor's office, their revolving door of providers that made it impossible to build trust and rapport, how some doctors wouldn't let me bring up more than one problem at once even if I thought they might be related, and so on. I feel like most people have probably experienced these same things at their doctor's office.

Forward provides a much better experience. The appointments last longer, up to an hour long for the annual check-up, and the doctors seem to take a more wholistic view of your health. I think they've been very helpful in managing some ongoing conditions I have. And when I had COVID, I was able to get a same-day virtual appointment with a doctor to discuss the pros and cons of Paxlovid given my risk profile, and when I decided to go ahead and take it, they were also able to send the prescription to my pharmacy.

That said, Forward isn't perfect. You won't always be able to see the same doctor, and sometimes you'll have to wait a few weeks to get an appointment. The text chat, while helpful, can at times be prone to miscommunications or answers that aren't optimally tailored to your specific needs. And at $149/person/month, the membership is quite expensive. Many people will be unable to afford it, and it's not a replacement for insurance because it won't help you at all if you end up in the hospital with a serious condition. In an ideal world, we'd have a better healthcare system and companies like Forward wouldn't make sense. But that's not the world we live in, and if you're not happy with your current doctor's office and you can afford it, then I think Forward is worth the money

LOVE IT!. Amazing so far! 

The simple novelty of having a doctor listen to you for MORE than 15 minutes is a luxury. I feel like every traditional doctor appointment I've had has been me rushing to explain my symptoms only to be dismissed by a condescending doctor with a response & diagnosis that could be bested by ChatGPT. 

The in-person experience is super slick and comforting, and I love being able to monitor my health on an ongoing basis via the app. I'm a bit of a hypochondriac, so having accessible healthcare at my finger tips is amazing. 

I used to work in the healthcare tech space, and this seems to be, by far, the best solution to America's healthcare crisis. I've only just started my subscription, but I'm super happy with the level of care thus far, as well as the seamless integration of their tech.

I genuinely feel like I am INVESTING in my ongoing health, and that's a great feeling to have

Most efficient GP Experience. Forward always provide the most efficient General Practice medical service I've experienced.  Their technology and their medical professionals, as well as their 24 chat service is the best

The good, the bad and the ugly. There is no waiting room. They do the the scan basically in a window front where everyone walking by in the mall can see your vitals and weight, which made me very uncomfortable. Isn't this a violation of HIPAA? I understand that it is a marketing tactic, but patients should be given the option from the first visit to use the machine they have behind closed doors. I expressed my concern when I saw the Dr and they said they would note my chart and use the machine in the back for future visits.

The Dr I saw in person was super nice and voiced her every move which made me very comfortable.  I liked how fast their basic labs results were available in about an hour. I had a test sent out to a lab that they have a contract with for discounted lab fees. Upon receiving the results, there was something indicative that I may need antibiotics so I told them to just send the script just in case, but they said NO and wanted me to do another out of pocket test with the lab they are affiliated with.  I called my regular GP who said this is ridiculous and she sent me the medication.  She said the test Forward wanted me to get was a waste of time and money based on the original results and them saying it "may go away on it's own" was far fetched and it would have been more proactive to write the script. Obviously this is just another ploy to send more business to Quest and waste everyone's time and more money.

It appears Forward has partnered up with Quest and 23andMe which is good and bad. Good in the sense that Quest supposedly gives you a discount on their lab fees, I guess I'll find out when I get the bill and compare it to their price list.  They only use 23andMe to take your raw DNA data although they can get the same raw data from Ancestry in the same manner. Do you see where I'm going with this? (This is why there's a direct link from the forward app to 23andMe) I previously uploaded my raw data from Ancestry to Genetic Genie and Promeatheas before joining Forward and got the same results when I recently uploaded my 23andMe. Now I have to wait 3 weeks for Forward's virtual appointment to get their genetic analysis of my 23andMe although I already know which variants I have, but curious how they will approach it.

Forward can't handle all my meds like my GP, which makes no sense of even getting this membership. The Dr from my baseline visit suggested I try a different medication, which was an epic failure for me and no one has even contacted me to follow up to see how I was doing on the med.  Crazy right?

My overall opinion of Forward is they use techy and gimmicky tactics to entice you to join. I can get the same care from my GP (minus instant labs) or even an online Dr (minus med management) for a fraction of the cost.  Depending on how my next appointments go, will decide whether I will continue with their membership and will update my review accordingly

I can't say enough how much I really…. I can't say enough how much I really appreciate the service. Dangerously Incompetent. Dangerously incompetent care. Stay away. Let me explain… I signed up for Forward with great hope and enthusiasm. It seemed a fantastic option for “Medicine 3.0” where technology and lifestyle management combine to help prevent the common causes of illness and injury. Core to this approach is intelligent use of blood labs to monitor a variety of bio markers that are not commonly tracked by most doctors. Not only did my 45-minute baseline visit not include MANY of these labs (I had to ask for them to be added, and the physician obliged) but when it actually came to scheduling the blood draw that’s when the REAL trouble ensued… I was told by a customer service rep before I signed up that all blood draws could be done at home since I don’t live near a physical office. NOPE. That was just a straight up lie. They don’t do that. Then I asked for lab scripts so that I could go to a Quest Diagnostics center and have the blood drawn there. It took FOUR days for them to get me the scripts. And when I got them… The scripts were for only TWO of the EIGHT tests the doctor had ordered. When I raised the problem with staff they said “oh we don’t know what happened but we’ll sort it out.” It took them another DAY to get the full battery of tests… and they were wrong AGAIN! They had erroneously replaced two critical tests with tests that were never ordered and wouldn’t be helpful. If I hadn’t been such a close reader of the lab scripts I would never have noticed this - I would have just gone to the lab and gotten the wrong tests. Would my doctor have ever noticed? I doubt it. How can you render competent, safe care when you can’t even quality control the blood labs that you are basing your care on? This service was completely inept. It’s dangerous. Stay away

Scammed me out of a entire year's membership because I was diagnosed with cancer. I was about 80% of the way through my one-year Parsley Health membership—which included five visits with a health coach and five with a doctor—when I was diagnosed with cancer. My life was completely turned upside down. After undergoing multiple surgeries, I realized my one-year membership was about to expire, so I reached out to ask what options were available to me.

I misread a somewhat confusing email response and believed they were advising me to schedule my remaining appointments before the one-year window closed. I did exactly that and completed the full ten visits - the ones I had already paid for.

Afterward, I discovered that Parsley Health had charged me for a second year of membership, which I neither wanted nor needed. Although they applied a small discount, I was still charged hundreds of dollars for a second year of care that I did not use. I only attended the appointments included in my original membership.

When I raised this issue, I was told they had to charge me because “they are a business.” If a healthcare company cannot show flexibility or compassion for someone going through cancer, it is not worth working with

I was a Parsley Health member for 2…. I was a Parsley Health member for 2 years, drawn to their holistic approach to healthcare. Unfortunately, the reality fell far short of what's promised for a premium healthcare membership.

Major Issues:
Provider Continuity Problems: I had two different doctors leave during my membership, forcing me to restart my care from scratch each time - rebuilding relationships, re-explaining my complete health history, and re-establishing treatment goals. Critical information gets lost in these transitions. My most recent doctor completely forgot to order my lipid panel despite cholesterol monitoring being a primary reason I joined Parsley. A provider who truly knew my health profile would never miss something so fundamental to my care plan.

Scheduling Difficulties: Consistently struggled to get timely appointments. I later learned this was partly due to understaffing issues - unacceptable when you're paying premium prices for healthcare access.

Administrative Problems: Multiple billing errors, credits not properly applied, and incomplete lab orders requiring follow-up corrections.

The Final Straw: When my membership was ending, a team member reached out asking for feedback and offering a 15% discount. I sent a detailed, thoughtful response outlining all these issues and asking if a more substantial offer might be possible given the service failures. I never received a response.
This non-response perfectly encapsulates Parsley's core problem: lack of genuine follow-through and member care. When a patient takes time to provide honest feedback about their healthcare experience, complete silence speaks volumes about how much the company actually values its members.

Parsley Health has a compelling philosophy and approach, but the execution - particularly around provider stability and consistent care delivery - makes it impossible to recommend at this price point. You're paying premium prices for what ends up being fragmented, inconsistent care
